advantages: No explicit training phase beyond storing data — simple to implement and update by appending new labeled points.
Flexible nonlinear decision boundaries without hand-designed basis functions — shape follows local data density.
Naturally handles multi-class classification via majority vote without one-vs-rest reformulation.
Interpretable predictions: show the k neighbor examples and their labels or targets to explain a decision.
Works for both classification (vote) and regression (neighbor average) with the same distance + k framework.
Strong baseline on small-to-moderate tabular datasets when features are scaled and dimensionality is manageable.
disadvantages: Slow inference at scale: naive prediction is O(n·d) per query unless spatial indexes or approximate NN are used.
High memory footprint — must retain the full training set for exact k-NN.
Sensitive to irrelevant features and feature scale — distance dominated by large-magnitude columns without preprocessing.
Curse of dimensionality: in high d, distances become less meaningful and all points appear similarly far.
k must be tuned; wrong k yields overfitting (small k) or oversmoothing (large k) — see Model Complexity.
Poor extrapolation: predictions stay within the envelope of training labels/targets in feature space — no global trend beyond local neighborhoods.
